questions.prompt: Which access order defines a stack?

answers.text: Push and pop normally operate at the same end.

feedback.legit: Push and pop normally operate at the same end. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Push and pop normally operate at the same end. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: Last in, first out.

feedback.legit: Last in, first out. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Last in, first out. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: First in, first out describes a queue.

feedback.legit: First in, first out describes a queue. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: First in, first out describes a queue. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

questions.prompt: Which access order defines a basic queue?

answers.text: First in, first out.

feedback.legit: First in, first out. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: First in, first out. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: The newest item always leaves first.

feedback.legit: A queue removes the oldest enqueued item first. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: A queue removes the oldest enqueued item first. You were right not to trust that reply.

answers.text: Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends.

feedback.legit: Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends. The original wording holds up.

questions.prompt: What prerequisite does ordinary binary search need?

answers.text: The target must be present.

feedback.legit: Binary search also terminates correctly when the target is absent. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: Binary search also terminates correctly when the target is absent. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: Binary search always runs in O(log n) time on a linked list.

feedback.legit: Finding the middle of a basic linked list is not constant time, so the usual array bound does not apply. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Finding the middle of a basic linked list is not constant time, so the usual array bound does not apply. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used.

feedback.legit: The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: Collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time.

questions.prompt: When does breadth-first search find shortest paths?

answers.text: It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph.

feedback.legit: It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: It directly handles arbitrary negative edge weights.

feedback.legit: Weighted shortest paths require an algorithm suited to those weights. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Weighted shortest paths require an algorithm suited to those weights. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source.

feedback.legit: It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source. The evidence supports the original reply.

questions.prompt: What does stability mean for a sorting algorithm?

answers.text: The algorithm never uses extra memory.

feedback.legit: Stability and memory usage are separate properties. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Stability and memory usage are separate properties. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Every implementation of quicksort is stable.

feedback.legit: Quicksort is not inherently stable; stability depends on the algorithm and implementation. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Quicksort is not inherently stable; stability depends on the algorithm and implementation. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: Equal-key items keep their original relative order.

feedback.legit: Equal-key items keep their original relative order. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Equal-key items keep their original relative order. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: What does a binary min-heap guarantee?

answers.text: Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time.

feedback.legit: Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: The minimum element is at the root.

feedback.legit: The minimum element is at the root. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: The minimum element is at the root. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: Every level is globally sorted from left to right.

feedback.legit: A heap only guarantees the parent-child order property. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: A heap only guarantees the parent-child order property. You were right not to trust that reply.

questions.prompt: What problem does a disjoint-set union structure solve?

answers.text: It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component.

feedback.legit: It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: It returns the shortest weighted path between two vertices.

feedback.legit: Union-find tracks connectivity, not path length. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Union-find tracks connectivity, not path length. That was correctly classified as Sus.

answers.text: Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time.

feedback.legit: Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time. Your Legit vote was right.

questions.prompt: When is dynamic programming a useful approach?

answers.text: Whenever recursion appears, regardless of repeated work.

feedback.legit: Recursion alone does not imply that dynamic programming is helpful. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Recursion alone does not imply that dynamic programming is helpful. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: Dynamic programming always chooses the locally best option at each step.

feedback.legit: Choosing a locally best option describes a greedy strategy, not dynamic programming in general. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: Choosing a locally best option describes a greedy strategy, not dynamic programming in general. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present.

feedback.legit: When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present. This one should stay marked Legit.

questions.prompt: Can standard Dijkstra's algorithm handle negative edge weights?

feedback.sus: Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: Yes, as long as there is no negative cycle.

feedback.legit: A negative edge can invalidate Dijkstra's greedy finalization step. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: A negative edge can invalidate Dijkstra's greedy finalization step. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed.

feedback.legit: Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed. The evidence supports the original reply.

questions.prompt: When does a topological ordering exist?

answers.text: For a directed acyclic graph.

questions.prompt: What do red-black tree invariants provide?

answers.text: They force the tree to be perfectly balanced.

feedback.legit: Red-black trees allow controlled imbalance. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Red-black trees allow controlled imbalance. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: Every root-to-leaf path has exactly the same total number of nodes.

feedback.legit: Red-black invariants equalize black height, not the total node count of every path. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: Red-black invariants equalize black height, not the total node count of every path. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Red-black tree lookup takes O(1) time in the worst case.

feedback.legit: The height is logarithmic, so lookup is O(log n) in the worst case. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: The height is logarithmic, so lookup is O(log n) in the worst case. Your vote matches the correction.
